---
title: "The data-state view"
description: "Advanced: how the pipeline is organized by the state of the data — for pipeline authors."
order: 70
---

There are two ways to read the pipeline. [Pipeline architecture](architecture.qmd)
reads it **by stage** — ingestion, processing, integration, downstream — which is
how an analyst thinks. This page reads it **by the state of the data**, which is
how the workflows are actually organized. If you are writing or wiring components,
this is the view that tells you where a step belongs.

## The data changes shape as it flows

The unit of data is not constant through the pipeline. It passes through four
states, and a component only makes sense in one of them:

1. **Single-sample** — one H5MU per sample, straight from ingestion. Each sample
   stands alone; there is no `sample_id` yet and nothing has been normalized.
2. **Multi-sample (concatenated)** — every sample joined into one object, with a
   `sample_id` column in `.obs` recording each cell's origin.
3. **Merged (multimodal)** — within a sample or cohort, modalities are split out
   for per-modality processing and then merged back into one multimodal object.
4. **Integrated** — a batch-corrected embedding plus Leiden clusters and a UMAP,
   produced by a separate integration workflow.

## Why the state matters

Some operations are only correct in a particular state:

- **Per-sample by nature** — QC and filtering (empty droplets, mitochondrial
  fraction, doublet detection) reason about one sample's own distribution, so
  they run in the **single-sample** state.
- **Cohort-wide by nature** — normalization, highly-variable-feature selection,
  and PCA need to see variation across the whole cohort, so they run only after
  concatenation, in the **multi-sample** state.

This is why you see the `_singlesample` / `_multisample` suffixes throughout the
codebase: they are not arbitrary names, they declare which data state a workflow
operates on.

| State | Example workflows | Operates on |
|---|---|---|
| Single-sample | `rna/rna_singlesample`, `prot/prot_singlesample` | one sample, one modality |
| Multi-sample | `rna/rna_multisample`, `prot/prot_multisample` | the concatenated cohort |
| Merged | `multiomics/process_batches` (via `dataflow/merge`) | all modalities together |
| Integrated | `integration/harmony_leiden`, … | the corrected embedding |

## The state transitions

The `multiomics` workflows exist to move data between states, so you rarely wire
these transitions by hand:

- **`process_singlesample`** runs the per-sample work while the data is still one
  H5MU per sample.
- **`dataflow/concatenate_h5mu`** performs the single-sample → multi-sample
  transition, adding `sample_id`.
- **`process_batches`** runs the multi-sample work per modality
  (`dataflow/split_modalities` → `*_multisample` → `dataflow/merge`), and, for
  large cohorts, does it on groups of samples in parallel before combining —
  capping peak memory.
- **`process_samples`** orchestrates the whole single-sample → multi-sample →
  merged sequence in one command.

Integration then takes the merged object and produces the integrated state; it is
run as a [separate workflow](architecture.qmd), once per cohort.

## What this means when you author a component

Before adding a component to a workflow, decide which state it reads:

- Does it reason about a **single sample** (so it belongs in a `_singlesample`
  workflow, before concatenation)?
- Or does it need the **whole cohort** (so it belongs after concatenation, in a
  `_multisample` workflow)?

That single question tells you where the component slots in, which assumptions
hold (`Is sample_id present? Is the data normalized yet?`), and which dataflow
utility (`concatenate_h5mu`, `split_modalities`, `merge`, `split_h5mu`) you need
around it. See [Creating pipelines](../contributing/creating-pipelines.qmd) for
the mechanics of wiring one in.
